1: The angelic guide brings Ezekiel into the nave (heikhal, H1964, the main hall of the temple). The doorpost measurement of six cubits on each side — using the long cubit of Ezekiel 40:5 (7 handbreadths = c.52.5 cm). Solomon's temple had the same tripartite structure: vestibule (ulam), nave (heikhal), and inner sanctuary (devir), though with slightly different proportions (1 Kings 6:2-3: nave 40×20×30 cubits). Ezekiel's temple is both larger and more precisely specified than Solomon's, representing an idealized sacred space rather than a measured copy of the historical temple.
2: The entrance to the nave: ten cubits wide with five-cubit sides. The nave itself is forty cubits long — identical to Solomon's nave (1 Kings 6:17). The consistency of the forty-cubit nave dimension between Solomon's temple and Ezekiel's vision suggests either that Ezekiel is working from the historical temple's proportions or that forty cubits was understood as the standard for the divine dwelling's main hall. Twenty cubits wide at its other wall: identical to Solomon's dimensions (1 Kings 6:2).
3: The guide enters the inner sanctuary alone — Ezekiel remains outside while measurements are taken inside. This corresponds to the fact that only the high priest entered the Holy of Holies, once a year on Yom Kippur. The inner entrance: two-cubit doorpost, six-cubit entrance, seven-cubit sides. The two-cubit doorpost is narrower than the outer doors, compressing access as holiness increases inward. The progressive narrowing of doorways as one moves from the outer court to the inner sanctuary is a spatial representation of increasing holiness.
4: 'He measured the inner room's length: twenty cubits; and its breadth: twenty cubits — the room in front of the nave' — the inner sanctuary (devir, H1687, the Holy of Holies) as a perfect cube (20×20×20 cubits — the height is not specified here but is implied). Solomon's devir was also 20 cubits in each dimension (1 Kings 6:20). The perfect cube is the geometric form of absolute perfection: equal in all dimensions, no hierarchy of directions. The Holy of Holies as a perfect cube is the spatial form of YHWH's perfection (cf. the New Jerusalem in Revelation 21:16 as a perfect cube, 12,000 stadia in each dimension).
5: 'He measured the wall of the temple: six cubits thick; and the side chambers surrounding the temple: four cubits wide on every side' — the massive wall thickness (six cubits = c.3.15 m) providing both structural integrity for the multi-story structure and the symbolic weight of the boundary between the sacred space and the exterior. The side chambers (tzelaot, H6763, side rooms/ribs) surrounding the temple are attested in Solomon's building: 1 Kings 6:5-8 describes three-story side chambers around the outside of the nave and devir.
6: 'The side chambers were arranged in three tiers, thirty to each tier' — ninety side chambers in total. The side chambers set into offsets (tzela'ot) in the temple wall rather than fastened to it — the same construction method described in 1 Kings 6:6 (the wall was not directly attached to the temple structure to avoid violating the temple's integrity). The offsets gave each successive floor its structural support without inserting beams into the sacred wall.
7: 'The side chambers grew wider as they rose, because the winding stairway around the temple went upward' — the tapering wall offsets creating progressively wider floors at each level. This architectural feature (corbeled or stepped upper floors in a multi-story structure) is confirmed in ancient Near Eastern palace and temple architecture from both Egypt and Mesopotamia. The winding stairway (musab, H4141, turning/coiling staircase) circling the temple exterior is functionally similar to the spiral staircases in medieval towers.
8: 'I also saw the height of the temple complex all around: the foundations of the side chambers rose to a full reed — six cubits — to the joining' — the full reed (six cubits) as the height of the foundation platform on which the temple stood. This elevated platform is architecturally significant: ancient Near Eastern temples characteristically sat on elevated platforms (ziggurat-style) that created visual and spatial separation from the surrounding space. The elevated platform visually expressed the temple's status as the meeting point of heaven and earth.
9: 'The outer wall of the side chambers was five cubits thick, and the open space between the temple's side chambers and the priests' chambers was twenty cubits all around the house' — the open space (mazra, H5299/H4516, free space/passageway) between the side chambers and the priests' outer buildings creating a functional buffer zone. This separation ensured that no common structure directly abutted the temple's side chambers, maintaining the holiness boundary.
10: 'Between the chambers and the house was a twenty-cubit width all around on every side' — the twenty-cubit buffer is identical in width to the inner court's open spaces. The consistent twenty-cubit dimension throughout the temple complex creates a modular grid — architectural order expressing theological order. In ancient Near Eastern sacred architecture, the repetition of specific dimensions was often intentional and theologically significant.
11: 'The doors of the side chambers opened into the free space: one door facing north, another facing south; and the free space all around was five cubits wide' — the side chamber access via doors opening into the free space rather than directly to the exterior or the temple interior. This traffic flow prevented direct contact between the most holy and the ordinary: movement through the sacred space is controlled by a series of intermediate zones.
12: 'The building that faced the separate yard on the west side was seventy cubits wide; its walls were five cubits thick on every side, and ninety cubits long' — the 'separate yard' (gizrah, H1508, the area west of the temple, sometimes translated 'restricted area') is a zone west of the temple that serves as a separation buffer between the temple and the western outer wall. Its function is not specified beyond separation. The large western building (70×90 cubits) may represent storage or service facilities.
13: 'He measured the temple: a hundred cubits long. The separate yard and the western building with its walls also measured a hundred cubits' — the temple compound's key dimension of a hundred cubits in each direction. The temple proper (nave + devir = 60 cubits) plus the vestibule and walls totals to the hundred-cubit dimension. The western building and separate yard also equal a hundred cubits. This symmetry creates a perfect square sanctuary block.
14: 'The width of the temple's front face and the separate yard on the east side was also a hundred cubits' — confirming the square plan: the temple block is 100×100 cubits. The east-west and north-south dimensions are equal. This perfect square plan reflects the same geometrical perfection as the Holy of Holies (20×20 cubits). Each nested space within the temple is geometrically perfect: the whole inner court (100×100), the Holy of Holies (20×20), and now revealed to be related proportionally.
15: 'He measured the length of the western building, facing the separate yard behind the temple, along with its galleries on each side: a hundred cubits' — the galleries (attiqim, H862, colonnade/gallery) running along the western building. These colonnaded walkways are attested in ancient Near Eastern palace architecture and provided covered outdoor space for processional routes and administrative functions. The measurement confirming the a-hundred-cubit dimension ties the entire western zone to the same modular system as the temple block.
16: 'The thresholds, recessed windows, and galleries — all three stories facing the threshold — were paneled with wood all around' — comprehensive wooden paneling covering all interior surfaces from threshold to windows to galleries. Solomon's temple was also comprehensively paneled in cedar (1 Kings 6:15-18: walls, floors, and ceilings all cedar). The wood covering created an entirely organic interior surface, eliminating the stone's hardness. The three-story gallery facing the threshold confirms the vertical organization of the side-chamber complex.
17: 'Above the doorway, in the inner room, and on the outside, and on all the walls inside and out — all was measured' — the measurement confirming total coverage of the decorative program. The inside/outside consistency of the decoration means the entire temple structure is covered with the cherubim-and-palm motif, not merely selected surfaces. The guide's comprehensive measurement creates an authoritative record of what was seen.
18: 'The paneling was carved with cherubim and palm trees: a palm tree between each pair of cherubim, and each cherub had two faces' — the specific decorative program. The alternating cherub-palm-cherub pattern is attested in Phoenician ivory carvings from Samaria (9th century BCE) and Nimrud (9th-8th century BCE), confirming that Ezekiel's vision reflects the actual decorative conventions of Phoenician-influenced Israelite temple art. The palm tree (tamar, H8558) was the tree of life in ancient Near Eastern iconography — the alternating cherubim and palms create a visual paradise-garden surrounding the divine dwelling.
19: 'Each cherub had a human face toward the palm tree on one side and the face of a young lion toward the palm tree on the other side' — the two-faced cherubim of the temple interior differ from the four-faced cherubim of Ezekiel 1 and 10. The temple decoration uses a simplified two-face version (human + lion) rather than the full four-face theophanic vision. The human face and lion face represent the two extremes of the animate world: rational humanity and wild animal power, both oriented toward the life-tree. This iconographic program reflects the ancient understanding of the temple as the meeting point of the entire created order in YHWH's presence.
20: 'From floor to above the doors, the walls were carved with cherubim and palm trees' — the vertical extent of the decorative program: floor-to-ceiling coverage. This comprehensive approach to sacred decoration is paralleled in Egyptian temple decoration (walls entirely covered with hieroglyphic texts and relief carving) and Mesopotamian palace decoration (orthostatic slabs with narrative reliefs). The sacred space leaves no surface un-signified: every inch of wall speaks of the divine presence and the created order gathered before YHWH.
21: 'The doorposts of the nave were squared' — the square doorpost (mezuzah rebua'ah) as the architectural statement of the nave's character: precise, right-angled, unornamented (as opposed to the recessed door frames of the outer court). The squared doorpost is a functional frame for the entrance, whereas the outer court's doors had more elaborate framing. 'The appearance of the sanctuary's face matched that of the nave' — consistency of design language between the nave and Holy of Holies entrance.
22: 'There was a wooden altar, three cubits high and two cubits long, with its corners, base, and walls all of wood. He said to me, This is the table that is before the LORD' — the wooden altar-table before YHWH, identified immediately as a 'table' (shulchan) despite being called an 'altar' (mizbe'ach). This identification corresponds to the table of the presence/showbread that stood in the nave of both the Tabernacle (Exodus 25:23-30) and Solomon's temple (1 Kings 7:48). The Talmud (Yoma 21b) identifies this as the altar of incense; the ambiguity between 'altar' and 'table' in the text may reflect the dual function of the incense-and-bread presentation that occurred in the nave.
23: 'The nave and the Holy Place each had double doors' — the folding double-door system for both the nave and the inner sanctuary. Solomon's temple had similarly designed doors: 1 Kings 6:31-35 describes olive-wood folding doors with two leaves for the inner sanctuary and cypress-wood folding doors with four panels for the nave. The double-door arrangement allowed the doors to be opened wide for processional purposes and closed to narrow entrances for security and holiness restriction.
24: 'Each double door had two leaves — two swinging panels for each door' — the specific leaf construction: four panels per door opening (two double-leaf doors, each with two panels). This creates a maximum possible aperture when fully open and graduated closing options. The technical precision of the door construction reflects the overall concern for exact specification throughout the vision: every detail of the sacred space is precisely determined by divine instruction.
25: 'The doors of the nave were carved with cherubim and palm trees, matching the carvings on the walls' — the decorative continuity between walls and doors: the sacred space has no undecorated surfaces. 'A canopy of wood was on the front of the vestibule on the outside' — the wooden canopy or overhang at the entrance vestibule, providing shade and defining the transition zone from the courts to the temple interior. The vestibule entrance as a threshold space marked by the canopy.
26: 'Recessed windows and palm trees adorned both sidewalls of the vestibule and the side chambers of the house and the canopies' — the decorative program extending to the vestibule sidewalls, completing the comprehensive coverage. The recessed windows (atumot, H331, latticed/closed windows — the same term as in 40:16 for the outer court windows) filtering light while maintaining the visual rhythm of the palm-tree motif throughout. The temple vision closes this chapter with every surface uniformly decorated: cherubim, palms, windows — paradise imagery everywhere.
